topic: A student learns their scholarship application was denied
raw_output:
[story 1]

The email sat on the desk, stark white against the muted grey of the monitor. Elara stared at the line, her breath catching in a shallow rhythm. The carefully constructed composure she usually wore felt brittle, threatening to shatter under the weight of the rejection. She picked up a pen, the plastic cool against her fingertips, and traced the grain of the wood veneer of her desk. Her shoulders slumped, a slow, heavy descent that seemed to pull her further into the chair. She didn't move to close the laptop. Instead, she let her gaze drift to the window, watching the relentless, predictable flow of traffic on the street below. A faint, almost imperceptible tremor ran through her hands, which she slowly laid flat on the keyboard, refusing to type, refusing to react.

[story 2]

"No," was the only sound that escaped Liam’s throat. He reread the rejection letter for the tenth time, the words blurring into meaningless shapes. He leaned back in his worn armchair, the springs groaning a low complaint beneath him. His gaze was fixed on a dust mote dancing in a shaft of afternoon sunlight slicing through the room. He didn't fidget; his stillness was absolute, a heavy inertia settling over him. He picked up a book, but his eyes refused to focus on the text; they simply tracked the edges of the pages. A deep, quiet sigh escaped him, a sound devoid of energy, as if his very muscles had decided to simply stop exerting effort.

[story 3]

Maya smoothed the crumpled paper on her lap, her movements slow and deliberate, almost ritualistic. The air in her small apartment felt thick, heavy, pressing against her skin. She didn't look at the computer screen displaying the notification; her attention was entirely focused on the way the light caught the dust motes settling on the windowsill. Her fingers curled loosely around the paper, a gesture of passive acceptance. When her roommate called her name, Maya turned her head with painstaking slowness, her expression carefully blank, as if the news had simply been a mild inconvenience, something to be cataloged and filed away without any real emotional response.
